front: DAG makespan with a single zero-delay edge
back: Do DFS to get a topo order, then forward DP: finish[u]=p[u]+max(0,best_in[u]); best_in[u]=max over preds w of finish[w]+c[w->u]. Track per node best1=(val1,pred1) and best2=val2. If zeroing edge (pred1,u), new_in=max(val2,0); reduction=val1-new_in. Largest reduction on a max-finish node. Complexity:
hint: Zero the incoming edge that currently powers the critical (max) node.
mnemonic: BEST1/BEST2 trick: track top two inbound values per node

front: Earliest Finish Time (EFT) in a DAG with setup times & critical path counts
back: Compute EFT[v] = d[v] + max_in(EFT[u] + s[u,v]); if none, EFT[v] = d[v]. count[v] = sum of counts of preds with max. Source count=1. Finish = max EFT. Distinct critical schedules = sum counts for nodes with EFT = Finish, mod 1e9+7. Time: O(N+M).
hint: Topologically order the DAG and propagate max finish times.
mnemonic: PATH: Paths Align Timings; Heaviest Timelines Count.

front: Trie with subtreeSum and subtreeBest for weighted prefix queries
back: Each trie node stores subtreeSum and subtreeBest=(word,weight). add(word,delta): traverse/create path, add delta to subtreeSum along it, update subtreeBest if weight rises or ties lexicographically. query(prefix): return subtreeSum and subtreeBest at that node. Edge cases: negative deltas, floor at
hint: Think of each node as a weighted hub tracking the best word under its prefix.
mnemonic: SUM-BEST: Sum, Update, Max, Best, Subtree, Tie.

front: Trie with per-node pass and end flags
back: Nodes have 26 children, int pass, bool end. insert(w): traverse/allocate, inc pass, set end. delete(w): ensure exists, dec pass along path, clear end, prune zeros. countPrefix(p): walk p; if missing 0; else return path.pass.
hint: Pass counts tell you how many words share a prefix.
mnemonic: PASS: Prefix Along Shared Steps

front: K-partition of array with squared-block-sum cost
back: Prefix sums give block sums in O(1). dp[i][k] = min_{0<=t<i} dp[t][k-1] + (pref[i]-pref[t])^2, with dp[0][0]=0. If N<K, no valid partition. Negative workloads are allowed; (sum)^2 is nonnegative. Top-down is the same memoized recurrence.
hint: Remember the last cut point and the square of the remaining block sum.
mnemonic: PSQ: Prefix sums, Square cost, Quick cuts.

front: In-place MSD radix sort on top 16 bits
back: Perform an in-place MSD radix pass on the top 16 bits using a stable three-way partition. Recurse on each bucket to sort by remaining bits. Merge by in-place rotation to preserve stability. Add a depth-limit switch to heap sort to guarantee O(n log n) worst-case.
hint: Start with the most-significant digits as buckets and keep all data moves in-place.
mnemonic: MAPS: Most-Significant Buckets; Arrange via 3-way in-place partitioning; Preserve stability; Switch to heap at depth cap

front: Streaming leaderboard: hashmap + lazy max-heap
back: Data: map task_id -> {score, ts}; heap: (score, task_id). On event: update map; push (new_score, id). Top-K: pop until heap entry matches map[id].score (lazy). Expire: ignore entries with ts < now-W; prune if desired. Complexity: update O(log N); top-K amortized O(log K).
hint: Validate heap entries against the map state when selecting top-K.
mnemonic: MAPS: Map, Adjust, Push, Scrub (lazy cleanup).

front: Two-pass external sort (external merge sort)
back: Pass 1: read up to M records, sort by (ts,id) with a stable in-memory sort, write runs to disk. Pass 2: k-way merge runs with a min-heap to output total order by (ts,id). Time ~ O(N log M) for sorts plus O(N log K) for merge; I/O ~ 2N reads/writes.
hint: Split into memory runs, then merge by (ts, id).
mnemonic: R-M-M: Read, Make runs, Merge

front: Stable multi-key sort (two-pass) for priority and time
back: Two-pass stable sort: first stable-sort by time, then stable-sort by priority. Final order is priority ascending, and within each priority, time ascending. Time: O(n log n); space: O(n) for a stable merge sort that preserves equal-key order.
hint: Sort by the least significant key first, then the major key
mnemonic: LSK-First: Time as least significant key, then Priority as most significant.

front: Segment Tree of Tries for prefix counting
back: Use a segment tree over indices. Each node stores a Trie with counts indicating how many strings in that interval pass through each node. To answer (L,R,P), visit the O(log N) covering nodes and for each Trie walk the P path to get a count; sum results. Handles empty results and overlapping prefixes
hint: sum counts from O(log N) segment nodes using a Trie per node.
mnemonic: STriE — ST (Segment Tree) + Trie for Prefix counts

front: Hybrid QuickSort-MergeSort (cache-friendly, in-place)
back: Hybrid in-place, cache-friendly sort: large subarrays use QuickSort (median-of-three pivot, larger partition first); small or depth-limited parts switch to bottom-up, in-place stable MergeSort using rotations. Depth capped at 2*log2(n). Delivers O(n log n) worst-case with good cache locality.
hint: Clue: large subarrays go to QuickSort, small ones to in-place stable MergeSort.
mnemonic: Q before M: Quick for big chunks, Merge for small chunks.

front: Max reward by deleting up to k non-overlapping palindromic substrings
back: Precompute P[l][r] indicating s[l..r] is palindrome in O(n^2). Let f(i, t) be the max reward from s[i:] using at most t deletions (memoized). Transitions: skip i -> f(i+1, t); delete any palindrome s[i..j] with P[i][j] -> (j - i + 1) + f(j+1, t-1). Base: i == n or t == 0 -> 0. For reconstruction, st
hint: Clue: look for palindrome blocks and build a index-deletions DP.
mnemonic: Mnemonic: PAL-DEL — Palindrome blocks, Add lengths, Limit by deletions

front: Trie with per-node counters: pass and end
back: Trie with per-node counters: pass and end. Insert(word) increments both; CountPrefix(prefix) returns prefix pass; Exists(word) if end>0. Erase(word) decrements along path if Exists, pruning zero-pass nodes. Duplicates tracked by counts; erase of missing word returns false. Time O(L); memory O(26L) w
hint: Walk the word through the trie and watch pass/end counters guide you.
mnemonic: PASS-END (Pass-through counts, End hits)

front: Median-of-three pivot in non-recursive 3-way quicksort (Dutch National Flag)
back: In-place, non-recursive 3-way quicksort using an explicit stack and Dutch National Flag (<, ==, >). Pivot uses median-of-three to avoid degeneracy; duplicates are grouped in the == partition. Push subarrays of length > 1. Time: average O(n log n); worst-case O(n^2). Space: O(n) for the stack (typica
hint: Partition into three zones and store subproblems on a stack.
mnemonic: MTP — Median pivot, Three-way, Push subproblems

front: Introspective 3-way quicksort with Dutch flag partition and heapsort fallback
back: In-place, cache-friendly sort: median-of-three pivot; 3-way partition (<, =, >) via Dutch national flag; depth > 2 log2(n) triggers heapsort. Worst-case time O(n log n); minimal data movement; auxiliary space O(log n) for recursion.
hint: Median-of-three pivot, Dutch flag partition, and heapsort fallback by depth.
mnemonic: M3D-H: Median-of-3, Dutch flag, Heapsort fallback

front: Per-user sliding-window top-K sum (streaming)
back: Per-user state: map UserID -> {sum, last_item, deque<(ts, value, item_id)>}. On event (u,i,v,ts): push to u's deque; while front.ts <= ts-W, pop and subtract its value from sum. last_item records last item. A global max-heap (sum, user) with lazy invalidation yields top-K in O(log K) per update.
hint: Clue: rolling-per-user sums with a shared top-K heap.
mnemonic: SUM — Sliding window, Unexpired items, Max-heap (lazy).

front: Monotone grid path counting with DP
back: Top-down: dfs(i,j) = 0 if grid[i][j] is 1; if at (n-1,m-1) return 1; otherwise memoize (i,j) and return dfs(i+1,j)+dfs(i,j+1). Bottom-up: if grid[i][j]==0 then dp[i][j] = (i==n-1&&j==m-1)?1:(i+1<n?dp[i+1][j]:0)+(j+1<m?dp[i][j+1]:0); else 0. Start/end blocked → 0. Time O(nm); Space O(nm).
hint: Remember: walls block a path; from each cell you can only go down or right and sum those options.
mnemonic: DR-DP: Down-Right DP for grid paths

front: In-place hybrid sort: quicksort3way with in-place stable merge
back: Use a three-way quicksort for partitions; on equal-key blocks, merge left and right in-place with rotations to keep stability. Use insertion sort for small subarrays. Address edge cases: all equal, already sorted, all unique; calibrate insertion-threshold s.
hint: Recall: split-rotate-merge for stable in-place sorting.
mnemonic: STIR — Split, Three-way, Insertion, Rotations

front: Constrained shortest path with limited B-edges (budgeted path)
back: Topologically order the A-DAG to compute distA via DAG DP. Build a layered graph with K+1 layers. For A-edge u->v, add (u,k)->(v,k) with cost 1. For B-edge u->v,w, add (u,k)->(v,k+1) with cost w. Run Dijkstra on the layered graph; optionally prune unreachable/subtrees to shrink exploration.
hint: Treat K as an extra dimension; B-edges consume a layer.
mnemonic: Layered budget graph: A keeps you on the same floor; B moves you up one floor.

front: Partition array into at most K batches with batch max cost
back: Bottom-up: dp[i][k] = min_{k-1≤j<i} dp[j][k-1] + max(a[j+1..i]); base dp[0][0]=0; others INF. Top-down: f(i,k) memoized, iterates j from k-1 to i-1, tracking max(a[j+1..i]). Edge cases: N==0 → 0; if K>=N → max(a); zeros → 0.
hint: Think in terms of partition points and batch maxima.
mnemonic: BATCH: Breaks And Tracks Contiguous Highs

front: External sort with RAM-limited chunks and k-way merge
back: RAM 256 MB. Chunk approx 128 MB; ~1.34M rec (100 B). N=1e9 -> R ~ 746. k ~ floor(256MB/4KB) = 65536, so all runs merge in one pass. Passes = 2. I/O ~ 400 GB (read input, write runs, read runs, write final).
hint: Think in terms of runs and a single merge pass.
mnemonic: R2M - Run-Then-Merge

front: Partition array into target-sum contiguous blocks (DP)
back: DP counts partitions of nums into contiguous blocks whose sums equal target, mod 1e9+7. Top-down: f(i)=sum f(j+1) for j≥i with sum(nums[i..j])=target; prune when cumulative sum>target; memoize by i. Bottom-up: dp[0]=1; for i from 0 to n-1 extend j while sum≤target; if sum==target then dp[j+1]=(dp[j+
hint: Transition only at indices where a block sum hits target.
mnemonic: TAP — Targeted Array Partitions

front: K-segment DP with Li Chao
back: dp[k][j] = min_{i<j} dp[k-1][i] + (S[j]-S[i])^2; S is prefix sums, S[0]=0. Init: dp[1][j] = S[j]^2. Use Li Chao: lines L_i(x)=(dp[k-1][i]+S[i]^2) + (-2 S[i]) x; query at x=S[j].
hint: Treat each i as a line; query the Li Chao tree at S[j].
mnemonic: LINES: Lines In, Notation Equals Squared-cost optimization

front: Hybrid stable sort: introspective QuickSort with depth limit + stable MergeSort
back: Start with introspective QuickSort with depth limit floor(log2(n)); median-of-three pivot and 3-way partitioning for duplicates. Monitor balance and run-length; if unbalanced or long runs occur, switch to a stable MergeSort using a single auxiliary buffer. Guarantees O(n log n) worst-case, O(n) spac
hint: When QuickSort hits duplicates or imbalance, switch to MergeSort.
mnemonic: HYBRID: Heuristic pivot; Yield on imbalance; Balance checks; Run-length aware; Introspective QuickSort; Duplicate-safe partitions.

front: Trie-based dynamic autocomplete with per-node balanced BST for topK
back: A Trie where each node stores subtreeSize and a balanced BST of (freq, word) for words under that prefix. On insert(word,freq): walk the path, inc subtreeSize and upsert (freq, word). On delete(word): dec. prefixCount(prefix) and topK(prefix,K) yield counts and K highest-frequency words (ties lexico
hint: Per-prefix top lists live in a BST at every Trie node.
mnemonic: TOP-K: Trie Of Prefix Keeps (top-K by freq, ties lexicographically)

front: Health-check driven load balancer with graceful drain
back: Implement a load balancer using active and passive health checks, maintain a per-server state machine (up, draining, down), buffer or gracefully drain in-flight requests during transitions, and automatically reintegrate recovered servers so no requests are dropped.
hint: Graceful drain keeps requests flowing during server state changes.
mnemonic: GRACE — Graceful drain; Reintegration of recovered servers; Active and passive health checks; Clear per-server state; Elastic load balancing.

front: K-way merge of sorted runs (r runs)
back: Detect r sorted runs in one pass. Put each run's head into a min-heap (value, run). Repeatedly pop the min, append to the output, and push the next element from that run. Time: O(n log r); Space: O(r). Edge cases: empty input yields empty output; all elements equal preserves stability; r=1 is alread
hint: Merge by taking the smallest current head from each run.
mnemonic: RUNS: Run Heads; Use Min-heap; Sweep.

front: Cache-aware block-hybrid sort
back: Partition into fixed-size blocks; sort each block in place with quicksort; merge adjacent blocks bottom-up in place using a buffer B sized to fit the cache (L1/L2). Time ≈ O(n log n) plus merge overhead; memory ≈ O(B); stability depends on the two steps and is not guaranteed unless enforced.
hint: Think in blocks and a tiny buffer that fits cache lines
mnemonic: BLOCK-MERGE: Block-sort, Locality-first, Overlap-merge with a Cache-sized buffer

front: Introsort (quicksort + heapsort) with depth-based switch
back: In-place introsort: start with quicksort (median-of-three pivot); depth limit = floor(2*log2(n)); if exceeded, switch to in-place heapsort for the rest. Use tail recursion to minimize stack. Not stable; O(log n) extra space; handles small arrays and duplicates.
hint: Depth limit triggers the heap switch.
mnemonic: QHID: Quick, Heapsort, In-place, Depth-limit.

front: Topological sort + Dijkstra on reachable subgraph with cycle check
back: Signature: shortestAndTopOrder(start) -> (topOrder, dist, hasCycle). Sketch: DFS from start to mark reachable nodes, detect cycles, and yield topo order for the DAG portion; then run Dijkstra from start to compute dist; hasCycle indicates cycles in reachable subgraph. With nonnegative weights, cycle
hint: Nonnegative weights imply the shortest path won’t rely on looping cycles.
mnemonic: TDC: Topo order, Distances, Cycle flag

front: Introsort (in-place) with median-of-three pivot and depth guard
back: Quicksort with a median-of-three pivot and in-place partition plus a depth limit floor(2*log2(n)). If depth exceeds the limit, switch to heapsort to guarantee O(n log n) worst-case. Time: O(n log n) worst-case; average O(n log n). Space: O(log n) due to recursion (heapsort path uses O(1) extra).
hint: Depth guard decides when to switch to a different sort.
mnemonic: INTROSORT = In-Place, N log N bound, Turn to Heapsort

front: Hybrid stable sort with cache-aware partitioning
back: Depth-limited quicksort on small, cache-friendly partitions plus a bottom-up, stable mergesort pass for large partitions. Preserves duplicates order; empty input returns empty. Worst-case time O(n log n); space O(n).
hint: Switch strategies at a cache-friendly partition size.
mnemonic: HYBRID: Hybrid Yields stable, Cache-friendly, Iterative, Dual-strategy

front: Dictionary-based string decomposition with DP (max score)
back: Bottom-up: dp[i] = max over len 1..maxLen of dp[i-len] + score[s[i-len:i]]; dp[0] = 0; dp[i] = -INF if no match. Return -1 if dp[N] is -INF. Top-down: f(i) = max over len 1..maxLen of score[s[i:i+len]] + f(i+len) with f(N) = 0. Edge: if no valid piece at a position, -1; overlaps resolved by max.
hint: Break s into dictionary pieces and sum their scores.
mnemonic: DP SCORE: Decompose, Pick max, Store (memoize), Optimize with DP, Recall best

front: 60s sliding-window dedup leaderboard (ring buffer)
back: 60-bucket ring buffer of per-second (u,p) pairs; last_seen[(u,p)]; per-page totals. On (u,p,ts): if ts-last_seen[(u,p)]<60, skip; else add to bucket[ts%60], totals[p]++, last_seen[(u,p)]=ts. Expire e=ts-60: for (u,p) in bucket[e], if last_seen[(u,p)]==e then totals[p]--, remove last_seen[(u,p)]. Top
hint: Think in rings: when a bucket expires, counts shrink.
mnemonic: RING: Ring buffer, Increment counts, Nix expired, Get top-K

front: Min path sum with one removal (two-state DP)
back: Two DP layers: P0 = best sum to (i,j) with no removal; P1 = best sum with removal used. Regular min-path transitions apply to P0; P1 also allows removing the current cell. Init P0[0][0]=grid[0][0], P1[0][0]=0. Answer = min(P0[end], P1[end]).
hint: Two-state DP: track before removal and after removal paths.
mnemonic: P0/P1 — two lanes: before vs after removal.

front: Trie-based autocomplete design (insert, startsWith, autocomplete)
back: Trie node with children map and a wordEnd flag. insert(word) O(len(word)). autocomplete(prefix, limit): reach end of prefix in O(len(prefix)); DFS to collect up to limit words in order. startsWith(prefix) O(len(prefix)); if none, return []. Memory: O(sum of word lengths); shared prefixes help, but l
hint: Follow the prefix path, then branch in sorted order to collect smallest matches.
mnemonic: TRIE = Think Roots In Every edge

front: Dijkstra: count of shortest s→t paths (mod 1e9+7)
back: Maintain dist[] (shortest) and ways[] (count of shortest s→t paths). Init dist[s]=0, ways[s]=1. For edge u→v with w: if dist[u]+w<dist[v], dist[v]=dist[u]+w; ways[v]=ways[u]. Else if dist[u]+w==dist[v], ways[v]+=ways[u]. All modulo 1e9+7. Answer: dist[t], ways[t] (0 if t unreachable). Zero-weight ed
hint: When relaxations tie on distance, add the source's paths to the target.
mnemonic: DW: Distances Win; Ways count.

front: Deadline-constrained single-machine scheduling (maximize value)
back: Choose a subset of tasks with (t, d, v) to start at 0 and finish by its deadline to maximize total value. Solve with DP: sort by deadline, treat time as capacity. Use top-down memoization (i, time) and bottom-up dp[i][time] with reconstruction. Handles infeasible sets, tight deadlines, and ties.
hint: Sort by deadlines, then DP over time to pack feasible tasks.
mnemonic: DPT — Deadlines First; Pack by Time; Total value

front: Hybrid insertion-merge sort with preallocated buffer
back: Use a top-down hybrid: for subarrays of length <= 16, sort in place with insertion sort. For larger ranges, split, sort halves, then merge into a single preallocated buffer of size n and copy back. Time O(n log n), extra space O(n).
hint: Hint: switch to insertion sort for tiny subarrays; merge uses one shared buffer.
mnemonic: SMIB: Small blocks Insertion, Merge with a Buffer.

front: Prefix-sum hashmap DP for K-sum blocks
back: Bottom-up: dp[i] = # ways to partition first i into blocks of sum K. Let P[i] be prefix sum; M[x] = sum dp[j] where P[j]=x. Init dp[0]=1, M[0]=1. For i=1..N: dp[i] = M.get(P[i]-K,0); M[P[i]] += dp[i]. Return dp[N] mod 1e9+7. Top-down: dfs(i)=sum dp(j) with P[i]-P[j]=K; memo on i; worst-case O(N^2).
hint: Focus on how prefix sums reveal valid cut points.
mnemonic: PK-MAP: Prefix Keys Mark Endpoints

front: Trie with endCount and subCount for kth lex word (duplicates allowed)
back: Trie: nodes store end and sub counts. Insert increments subCount along path and endCount at end; Delete decrements. countPrefix(p) returns subCount at node of p. kthWord(k): if k<=endCount return prefix; else k-=endCount; scan children a..z; descend into first with subCount>=k; if none, null.
hint: EndCount first, then explore lexicographic children by subCount.
mnemonic: ESL: End-first, SubCount, Lexicographic order

front: Augmented AVL multiset with per-node count, subtree_size, subtree_sum
back: An AVL-based multiset stores each key once with a multiplicity count; nodes track count, subtree_size (total items), and subtree_sum (sum). Insert increments count or inserts node; delete decrements or removes. Rotations update aggregates. Range queries querySum and queryCount run in log N.
hint: Hint: Imagine each key stored once with a counter; aggregates update during rotations.
mnemonic: COUNT-AVL: Count duplicates, Update sums, Navigate with Rotations, Track sizes, Validate balance.

front: MultiTagRollingTopK
back: Per (user_id,tag) deque of (ts,value) for last W seconds; per-user total across tags. Max-heap of (total,user) with lazy deletions. Ingest: append to deque, update total; expire items with ts <= now-W and adjust totals; push updated total. Top-K: pop valid tops until K found. Complexity: O(log N) pe
hint: Sliding time window plus a heap with lazy deletions.
mnemonic: DEAL — Deques, Expire, Aggregate totals, Lazy heap.

front: Distinct subsequences count (T in S)
back: Counts ways T occurs as a subsequence of S modulo 1e9+7. Bottom-up: 1D dp of length m+1, dp[0]=1; for c in S, for j from m down to 1, if c==T[j-1], dp[j]=(dp[j]+dp[j-1])%MOD. Return dp[m]. Time O(|S||T|), Space O(|T|). Handles empty T (1); empty S yields 0 unless T empty. Memoized top-down variant a
hint: Update from end to start to avoid overwriting needed values.
mnemonic: COTS: Count Of T subsequences

front: Shortest paths with blocked nodes: prune via DFS, Dijkstra, SP DAG DP
back: Outline: prune to unblocked reachable nodes with DFS; run Dijkstra on this subgraph to obtain dist[]. Build a shortest-path DAG with edges (u,v) where dist[u]+w(u,v)=dist[v], topologically sort, and DP to count distinct shortest paths modulo 1e9+7. If dist[t] is INF, no path.
hint: Prune to reachable unblocked nodes before applying the shortest-path DAG DP.
mnemonic: PDP = Prune, Dist, Path-count

front: Dutch Flag three-way partition quicksort (in-place)
back: Three-way in-place quicksort (Dutch flag): partition into <, ==, > pivot; recurse on < and >. Time O(n log m), space O(log n). Edge: all equal, sorted, no duplicates. Skeleton: q(a,l,h){p=a[(l+h)>>1];lt=l;i=l;gt=h;while(i<=gt){if(a[i]<p) swap(a[lt++],a[i++]);else if(a[i]>p) swap(a[i],gt--);else i++;
hint: Dutch flag helps with three-way partitioning.
mnemonic: DUTCH: Divide, Unify, Tail-recursion, Check equals, Handle duplicates

front: Per-sensor moving-window with top-K totals (lazy validation)
back: Per-sensor circular buffers hold recent events; a hashtable dedups (sensor, metric) within W; sensor_totals[s] sums non-expired values. A min-heap of size K stores (total, sensor) with lazy validation. On insert/expire, update totals and push; pop stale. Clock advances expiry; purge old events. Time
hint: Think rolling top-K with per-sensor history and a dedup window.
mnemonic: CDTOP: Circular buffers, Dedup, Totals, Top-K lazily, Expiry, Per-sensor

front: BFS-based shortest-path counting with blocked nodes
back: Run BFS from s on unblocked nodes. dist=INF, ways=0; dist[s]=0, ways[s]=1. For edge v->u (u unblocked): if dist[u] INF, dist[u]=dist[v]+1, ways[u]=ways[v]; else if dist[u]==dist[v]+1, ways[u]=(ways[u]+ways[v]) mod 1e9+7. Output dist[t], ways[t] (0 if unreachable or blocked).
hint: Focus on distance layers to accumulate path counts.
mnemonic: D-Way: Distance tells layers, Ways tells counts

front: Hybrid SequenceAnalytics: array + DLL + hash + max-heap
back: Hybrid SequenceAnalytics uses: an array for storage, a doubly linked list for O(1) insert/remove, a map value->freq, and a max-heap of (freq, value) with lazy invalidation for top-K. Timestamps on nodes enable expiration for sliding-window queries as new elements arrive.
hint: Think of a storage stack with a freq-driven crown and ticking clock.
mnemonic: A-F-H-E-D-L: Array, Frequency hash, Heap, Expiration, DLL

front: Obstacle-aware Fibonacci DP for staircase paths
back: Count ways to reach N from 0 using 1 or 2 steps while avoiding blocked steps. Use blocked[i] markers. If blocked[i], dp[i]=0; otherwise dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2] (mod 1e9+7), with dp[0]=blocked[0]?0:1 and dp[-1]=0. Bottom-up uses O(N) space. Top-down: solve(i)=0 if i<0; 1 if i==0; 0 if blocked[i]; else
hint: Think Fibonacci, but blocked steps reset counts to zero.
mnemonic: OB-FIB: Obstacles Block, Fib-like Growth.

front: Introsort: Quicksort with depth-based switch to heapsort
back: Introsort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case by tracking recursion depth and switching to heapsort when depth exceeds 2*floor(log2(n)). It uses 3-way partitioning to handle duplicates, tail-recursion optimization to reduce stack usage, and uses O(log n) auxiliary space. Not stable.
hint: Depth threshold triggers switching from quicksort to heapsort.
mnemonic: INTRO: In-depth QuickSort, Then Revert to Heap

front: Sliding-window dedup + per-city totals + top-K in streaming
back: Data: 30s event deque; recent[(driver_id,trip_id)]=ts for 30s dedup; cityTotal[city]=sum distances in last 300s; max-heap (total,city) with lazy validation for top-K. Expire by deque pops and adjust cityTotal. Ops: insert/update O(log N); top-K amortized O(K log N); mem O(N).
hint: Think tiny window, unique pair, and a lazy max-heap.
mnemonic: WHET — Window, Hash, Evict, Top-K

front: Trie with per-subtree top-k by frequency
back: Trie where each node stores an ordered set of (−freq, word) for all words in its subtree, plus a global freq map. insert/delete update freq and propagate along the path root→terminal. query(prefix, k) moves to the prefix node and returns first k entries. Complexity: update O(|word| log W); query O(|
hint: Rank subtree words at each node by (−freq, word) to pick top-k.
mnemonic: FREQ-TRIE: Frequency-ranked Entries in each subtree.

front: Dutch National Flag three-way quicksort
back: An in-place three-way quicksort partitions around a pivot into <, ==, > regions, then recursively sorts only the < and > parts. The == region is left in place, improving duplicates handling. It yields average O(n log n) time with O(1) extra space.
hint: Partition around a pivot into three regions: <, =, >; duplicates ride in the middle.
mnemonic: DNF = Dutch National Flag; remember: <, =, > zones.

front: Topological DP for DAG shortest paths
back: DP on DAG: build graph, compute Kahn topo order, then relax edges in topo order from source (dist[s]=0, others INF). If topo length < n (cycle), fall back to Dijkstra. Unreachable nodes yield -1. Time: O(n+m) DAG, else O((n+m) log n). Space: O(n+m).
hint: Topo order provides a single-pass relaxation schedule.
mnemonic: TOPO: Topological Order Propagates Outcomes

front: Earliest arrival time in a weighted service-call graph
back: dist[] initialized to inf; dist[src]=0; use adjacency list. Run Dijkstra with a min-heap to relax dist[v] = min(dist[v], dist[u] + w). DFS/BFS for reachability. If DAG, topological sort + DP. Complexities: Dijkstra O((V+E) log V); reachability O(V+E); DAG DP O(V+E).
hint: Clue: frame as a weighted shortest path with reachability checks.
mnemonic: D3: Distances, Discover reachability, DAG DP

front: Circuit Breaker in Load Balancer
back: Circuit breaker with configurable failure thresholds, timeouts, and rolling success rate. When a backend health degrades, traffic is blocked; in half-open, a small probe checks recovery; it closes when health stabilizes. Metrics: failure rate, consecutive failures, timeouts, and recent success rate.
hint: Treat it like a fuse that trips on failures and tests recovery.
mnemonic: OPEN: Observe health, Pause traffic, Evaluate with a probe, Normalize traffic.

front: Exact-k grid path DP with diagonal moves
back: State (i,j,t) = max sum to reach (i,j) in t moves. Recurrence: dp[i][j][t] = grid[i][j] + max(dp[i-1][j][t-1], dp[i][j-1][t-1], dp[i-1][j-1][t-1]); bounds guard. Memoized DFS caches subproblems; bottom-up fills t=1..k. Time O(N*M*k); Space O(N*M*k) naive; memory-optimized: roll two 2D layers to O(N*
hint: Think of adding a layer per move and taking max of predecessors.
mnemonic: DIM = Diagonal, Increment, Max (layers show each move)

front: 3-way quicksort (Dutch national flag) with randomized pivot
back: Partition into three regions around a random pivot: < pivot, == pivot, > pivot. Recursively sort < and >; keep == in place. This reduces comparisons when duplicates occur by grouping duplicates together.
hint: Three-region partition around a pivot, like the Dutch flag colors
mnemonic: LEG: Less, Equal, Greater

front: Rolling-window leaderboard with dedup and top-K
back: Circular buffer of W buckets stores events; a map from (userId, videoId) tracks (ts, duration) to dedupe within window; per-video totals update on add/expire; maintain a fixed-size top-K (min-heap) updated on changes; getTopK(K) returns leaders; memory O(W + distinct pairs + K).
hint: Think circular buffers and a small top-K heap.
mnemonic: WAVE: Windowed Aggregation with Video Entries

front: Topological Sort (Kahn's algorithm)
back: Kahn's algorithm: compute in-degrees, enqueue 0-degree nodes, pop to build the topological order, and decrement neighbors; if count < n, return null (cycle). BFS from a root for unweighted shortest paths: dist[root]=0; dist[v]=dist[u]+1; -1 for unreachable. Edge cases: cycle, empty graph, isolated n
hint: Hint: Track in-degrees and use a zero-degree queue.
mnemonic: KAHN — Keep Adding, Halt when None

front: Top-K Frequent Elements in a Data Stream
back: Counts map: x -> freq. A size-K min-heap of (freq, value) with ties by smaller value. On add(x): freq[x]++. If x in heap, adjust; else if heap size < K push; else if freq[x] > minFreq or (freq[x] == minFreq and x < minValue) replace min. getTopK() returns up to K by freq desc, value asc.
hint: Clue: keep the leaders with a tiny heap and a frequency map.
mnemonic: TOP-K: Track, Order, Pick; ties go to smaller value.

front: FreqMax: stream freq counter with getMaxFreq(), tie-break smallest value
back: Hash counts[x] and a max-heap of (count, -x) with lazy deletions. add(x): counts[x]+=1; push (counts[x], -x). getMaxFreq(): while heap[0][0] != counts[-heap[0][1]]: pop; return -heap[0][1].
hint: Frequency guides the top; ties resolve to the smallest value.
mnemonic: FREQ: Frequency, Equal-tie goes to Smallest, Lazy deletions

front: Missing-Duplicate detection via in-place cyclic sort
back: Algorithm: in-place cyclic sort by swapping A[i] with A[A[i]-1] while 1 <= A[i] <= n and A[A[i]-1] != A[i]. Then scan for first i with A[i] != i+1; missing = i+1, dup = A[i]. Time O(n); Space O(1). Edge cases: handles one missing and one duplicate.
hint: Place each value at index-1, then scan for the first mismatch.
mnemonic: MD-SORT: Missing, Duplicate, Sort

front: Word Break with top-down memoization (countWays)
back: Top-down: countWays(i) = number of segmentations of s[i:] using dict. For len 1..maxWordLen, if s[i:i+len] in dict, add countWays(i+len); memoize by i. Bottom-up: dp[n]=1; for i from n-1 down to 0, dp[i] = sum of dp[i+len] over valid words.
hint: Cache results to avoid recomputation.
mnemonic: MEMO: Memorize subproblems; Enumerate options.

front: Sliding-window dedup + per-user top-K scoring (streaming)
back: Deduplicate by storing latest ts per (user_id, action) within window W. Maintain per-user totals and per-user event lists to expire old events. Use a global max-heap of (total_score, user_id) with lazy invalidation to fetch top-K in O(log K) per update.
hint: Clue: a sliding window, per-user sums, and heap with lazy invalidation
mnemonic: DASH — Dedup, Aggregate, Store, Heap (lazy)

front: Partition array into <= K subarrays to minimize sum of (max-min)
back: Top-down DP(i,r) = min over t>=i cost(i,t) + DP(t+1,r-1); base DP(N,0)=0, DP(i,0)=INF. Precompute cost(i,t)=max(A[i..t])-min(A[i..t]); store choice to reconstruct breakpoints. Bottom-up variant fills DP and parent pointers similarly.
hint: Precompute subarray costs and store split decisions to reconstruct.
mnemonic: BRK: Breakpoints Reveal Key

front: Introsort: depth-limited hybrid of quicksort and heapsort
back: Run quicksort with a depth limit of 2*log2(n). When the limit is reached, switch to heapsort on the current subarray to guarantee O(n log n) worst-case. Use in-place Hoare partitioning and an explicit stack to manage subproblems and tail recursion.
hint: When the recursion depth grows too deep, switch gears.
mnemonic: INTROSORT — In-Place, N log N, Turn to Heap when depth hits.

front: TopK Trie: per-node top phrases by freq and lexicographic order
back: Trie node stores a topK list of phrases, sorted by frequency (desc) and lexicographic tie. insertPhrase updates counts along the path and reorders topK. topK(prefix,k) returns the prefix node's topK. Costs: insert O(L log K); query O(L+K). Edge cases: neg deltas, freq 0, missing prefix, K>size, memo
hint: Keep the top k at every node of the prefix tree.
mnemonic: TOPKIE — Top Phrases In Every node

front: Stable bucket sort by remainder (A[i] mod m)
back: Algorithm: Use m buckets keyed by remainder 0..m-1. First pass counts per remainder; compute start indices by prefix sums; Second pass place A[i] into its remainder bucket at the next free slot. This is O(N+m) time, O(m) space, and stable (input order preserved). Example: A=[14,3,21,4], m=7 -> final
hint: Hint: Group by remainder with m buckets, then concat buckets in ascending order.
mnemonic: MB-Sort — M Buckets, Stable Order

front: Trie with per-node AVL multisets for weighted prefix queries
back: Trie with AVL at each node for its subtree; a global word->weight map. On insert/update/delete, traverse the word and update every node’s AVL accordingly. query(prefix,k) returns the top-k by weight from the prefix node’s AVL. Time: updates O(len(word) log N); query O(log N + k). Space: O(sum word l
hint: Hint: Combine prefix search with a balanced tree of weights at each node.
mnemonic: WEIGHT: Weigh Every Word In Trie

front: Weighted polygon triangulation: min-cost DP with counts
back: DP[i][j] = min over i<k<j of DP[i][k]+DP[k][j]+w[i]*w[k]*w[j], with DP[i][i+1]=0 and Count[i][i+1]=1. Count[i][j] tracks number of optimal triangulations. Top-down memoization or bottom-up tabulation. Time: O(n^3); Space: O(n^2).
hint: Split the interval i..j at some k to combine subproblem costs.
mnemonic: W3DP — Weights Three-Product DP

front: Coin Change: number of unordered combinations (unbounded coins)
back: Two methods. Top-down: dfs(i, rem) with memo[(i,rem)]. rem==0 -> 1; i==n or rem<0 -> 0; recurse skip or use coin i. Bottom-up: dp[s] = ways to form s with unlimited coins. Edge: target==0 -> 1; empty coins. Time: O(n*target); Space: Top-down O(n*target); Bottom-up O(target).
hint: Remember this is combinations, not permutations, with unlimited coins.
mnemonic: CCU: Count Combos Unbounded

front: Minimum insertions to form a palindrome
back: Let dp[i][j] be the min insertions to make s[i..j] a palindrome. If s[i]==s[j], dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j-1]; else dp[i][j]=1+min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Base: dp[i][i]=0. Fill by increasing length; answer dp[0][n-1].
hint: Process substrings from short to long, comparing the ends at each step.
mnemonic: PAL-DP: Pair ends; If equal, Look inside; Else add one and take min(left,right).

front: Earliest Finish Time in a DAG (longest path)
back: Compute est[i] = d[i] for all i. Kahn's topological order: for edge u->v, est[v] = max(est[v], est[u] + d[v]). When in-degree[v] becomes 0, enqueue v. total = max_i est[i]. If fewer than N nodes are processed, a cycle exists; report an error.
hint: The critical path is the longest route from a source to a sink.
mnemonic: CP-LP: Critical Path = Longest Path in DAG

front: DFS with color marking for cycle detection and topological sort
back: Run DFS with color marks: White (unvisited), Gray (in progress), Black (done). If you see a Gray neighbor, return the cycle path from current to that neighbor. If no cycle found, return vertices in reverse finishing order as a valid topological sort.
hint: Detect cycles by revisiting a node that is currently in progress (gray).
mnemonic: WGB: White-Gray-Black colors guide DFS cycle detection and toposort

front: Trie-based auto-complete with per-node counters
back: Explanation: Each node tracks subtreeCount. insert(word) increments subtreeCount on the path from root to the end. getPrefixCount(prefix) walks to the prefix node and returns its subtreeCount, or 0 if the prefix path doesn't exist. Example: after 'cat' and 'cater', getPrefixCount('cat') = 2.
hint: Clue: tally how many words share the same starting path.
mnemonic: CATS: Count All The Shared-prefix Strings.

front: Subset Sum Count with DP (top-down & bottom-up)
back: Two DP methods to count subsets summing to S. Top-down: dfs(i,t)=dfs(i+1,t)+dfs(i+1,t-num[i]); memo(i,t); base t==0?1: i==n?0. Bottom-up: 1D dp; for x in nums, for w from S down to x: dp[w]=(dp[w]+dp[w-x])%MOD. Ans dp[S]. Zeros double counts. MOD=1e9+7. Time O(nS), Space O(S).
hint: Use include/exclude at each step; zeros double the count.
mnemonic: DP-SSC: Dynamic Programming Subset Sum Count

front: Augmented AVL Tree for dynamic multiset with rank and rangeCount
back: An AVL where each node stores key, freq, height, size. insert/erase adjust freq or insert/remove a node, then rebalance and update size up the path. rank(x) returns count of keys <= x; rangeCount(a,b) = rank(b) - rank(a-1).
hint: Think rank-based counts on a balanced BST with per-node freq/size.
mnemonic: KFHS-RR: Keep Key, Freq, Height, Size; Rank then Range.

front: EWMA-based Load Balancing with Cold-Start Ramp-Up
back: Track each server's latency with EWMA, updating after every response; route to the server with the smallest EWMA score and keep scores current as samples arrive. Cold starts: gradually ramp traffic to new servers (small initial share, progressive growth) while monitoring errors and tail latency.
hint: A moving average guides which server handles the next request.
mnemonic: EWMA = Exponential Weights Moving Averages

front: Windowed streaming dedup with per-service sums and top-K
back: Approach: dedupe (service,id) via last_ts; use a min-heap for expirations by ts; per-service sum map; max-heap with lazy invalidation for top-K. On event: if ts - last_ts > W, push expiry, add value to sum, update last_ts. Expire: pop expired and subtract. Top-K: O(log K).
hint: Clue: sliding window, dedupe by id, two heaps for sums and ranking.
mnemonic: DEST: Dedupe, Expire, Sums, Top-K

front: Dijkstra, path counts, and topological shortest-path subgraph
back: dist = distS[t]. Path count: cnt[t] mod 1e9+7. Reconstruct one path via a predecessor from s to t. Vertex on any s to t shortest path iff distS[v] + distToT[v] == dist (distToT from reverse). The tight edges distS[u] + w = distS[v] form a DAG for the topological view.
hint: A vertex on a shortest s to t path satisfies distS[v] + distToT[v] == dist.
mnemonic: Mnemonic: S+T=Total (S to T)

front: Lexicographic shortest path on a DAG with skip edges
back: Topological DP: keep best (hops, latency) per node from s with a predecessor. In topo order, for edge (u,v,w,skip) cand=(hops[u]+1, latency[u]+(skip?0:w)); if cand < (hops[v],latency[v]) lexicographically, update and pred[v]=u. Reconstruct path from t to s.
hint: Treat hops as the primary measure; latency only counts for non-skipped edges.
mnemonic: HOP-SKIP: Hops determine path; SKIP latency on flagged edges.

front: Dijkstra with K free passes
back: Run Dijkstra on expanded state (node, used) where used counts free passes. For edge (u,v,w): relax (v,used) by w; if used<K, relax (v,used+1) by 0. Store parents to reconstruct path. Time: O((VK+EK) log(VK)); mem: O(VK). Unreachable if no state reaches t; K=0 reduces to standard Dijkstra.
hint: Treat the count of used passes as part of the node state in Dijkstra.
mnemonic: FREE-K: Free edges; Track state; End with path.

front: LiveTopK: sliding-window top-k frequencies
back: Data structure: hashmap counts[word] for current window, queue of (ts, word) to expire, and a max-heap with lazy updates of (freq, word). add(w,ts): counts[w]++, push (counts[w], w) to heap. expire(ts): pop and adjust for (t,w) with t <= ts-W. topK(k): pop from heap, skip stale entries, collect k wo
hint: A sliding window with a lazy-heap keeps the top-k in sync.
mnemonic: SLOT: Sliding window, Lazy updates, Ordered by freq, Tie-break lexicographically

front: Windowed dedup + per-service sum + top-K
back: Deduplicate by (service,id) with last_ts; expire old events using a min-heap by ts (subtract value from service sum); keep service->sum; use a max-heap with lazy invalidation for top-K. On arrival: if not dup, push expiry, add to sum. On expiry: subtract. Top-K: pop valid tops.
hint: A sliding window with one recorded event per service-id.
mnemonic: D.E.S.T. — Dedupe, Expire, Sum, Top-K.

front: EWMA-based load balancer for dynamic routing
back: Use an EWMA of each server's response time to rank servers. Route requests to the lowest EWMA latency servers; progressively shift traffic toward the fastest ones. For cold starts, start with low traffic to new/slow servers and ramp up gradually as their EWMA stabilizes, avoiding saturation.
hint: Latency history guides traffic toward the fastest responders.
mnemonic: WEIGHT: Weigh Every Instance, Generate Higher Throughput.

front: Dijkstra with path counting and shortest-path DAG
back: Compute distS from s and distT to t (via reversed graph). D = distS[t]. Maintain cnt[v] = # shortest s→v paths (mod 1e9+7): during relax u→v with w, if dist[v] > dist[u]+w, set dist[v] and cnt[v]=cnt[u]; else if dist[v]==dist[u]+w, cnt[v]+=cnt[u]. To get one shortest path, follow edges with dist[u]+
hint: Think in terms of a DAG of edges on shortest paths.
mnemonic: DIST: DistS + DistT equals D; Identify S, T, and Topology

front: Lexicographic shortest path on a DAG with skip-edges (hops, latency)
back: Topological DP: keep for each node the best (hops, latency) from s and a predecessor. In topological order, relax edge (u,v) with weight w and skip flag: cand=(hops[u]+1, latency[u]+(skip?0:w)). If cand lexicographically beats best[v], update. Reconstruct path from t via predecessors. If a cycle exi
hint: Prioritize fewer hops; latency only matters when hops tie.
mnemonic: HLT — Hops Lead, Latency Tracks

front: Count subsets with sum S (0/1 knapsack counting)
back: DP counts subsets with sum S. Top-down: memoized DFS on (i,t); pick or skip nums[i]. Base: t==0 → 1; i==n → 0. Bottom-up: 1D dp; for a in nums, for w from S down to a: dp[w]=(dp[w]+dp[w-a]) mod 1e9+7. Zeros double counts. Answer = dp[S]. Time O(n*S); Space O(S).
hint: Each element is a 0/1 choice; count ways to sum to S.
mnemonic: Take or Toss DP (TOT-DP)

front: LiveTopK: streaming top-k with sliding window
back: LiveTopK uses a hashmap word->freq, a max-heap(freq, word) with lazy updates, and an expiration queue. add(word, ts): freq++ and push. expire(ts): drop items with ts<=ts-W; prune heap lazily. topK(k): pop k valid entries, ties lexicographically. Memory O(D); time ~ O(log D) per add/topK; expiration
hint: Expire old entries using ts-W and refresh the heap lazily.
mnemonic: LIFT: Lazy heap, In-window counts, Expire queue, TopK

front: Augmented AVL Tree for dynamic multiset with rangeCount
back: Use an AVL where each node stores key, freq (dup count), height, and size (subtree total elements). insert/erase adjust freq or insert/delete node, then rebalance and update height/size up the path. rank(x) counts keys <= x via left.size + freq plus recursion; rangeCount(a,b)=rank(b)-rank(a-1).
hint: Use rank to convert rangeCount into a prefix-difference query.
mnemonic: KFHS: Keys, Frequency, Height, Size

front: Shortest path with up to K zero-cost passes
back: Use Dijkstra on augmented state (node, used). For each edge (u→v,w): relax to (v,used) with cost+ w; if used < K, also relax to (v,used+1) with cost 0. Track parents to reconstruct path. Complexity: O((V*K+E*K) log(V*K)); memory: O(V*K). Handle unreachable targets and K=0.
hint: Model the search with states (node, used) to count free passes.
mnemonic: FK-Dijkstra: Free-K edges Dijkstra

front: Streaming top-K leaderboard with per-player scores and 1s dedup window
back: Approach: scores[pid] = {score,lastTs}; per-player list for recent events; dedupe with 1s window on (pid,ts); topK = min-heap of (score,pid) size K. On (p,d,t): if (p,t) in last 1s skip; else scores[p].score+=d; scores[p].lastTs=t; update list; adjust heap; return top-K. Time: O(log K) per event.
hint: Clue: watch the sliding 1s window and the min-heap for leaders.
mnemonic: SCORE: Scores, Check dedup window, Order top-K, Refresh output

front: Health-aware load balancer with health checks and failover
back: A load balancer that uses health checks and a circuit-breaker to remove failing servers from the active pool, buffers or drains in-flight requests, and reintegrates healthy nodes automatically, ensuring no requests are dropped during failover.
hint: Think of a traffic cop for servers that prevents bad nodes from handling requests.
mnemonic: HEAL: Health checks; Eliminate unhealthy; Activate pool; Load-balance with draining.

front: External-memory sort (two-phase)
back: Phase 1: read B items, sort in RAM, write a run; repeat for n. Phase 2: k-way merge with a min-heap, load up to k runs. I/O: Phase1 reads n, writes n; Phase2 reads n, writes n. Time: Phase1 O(n log B); Phase2 O(n log k). Pick B as large as RAM allows; pick k with B + k·block ≤ RAM.
hint: Think in terms of runs, buffers, and a heap.
mnemonic: RUN-BUF-HEAP: Run buffers heap merge.

front: In-place stable merge sort (O(n log n), O(1) space)
back: Recursively sort halves. Merge in place by rotating A[i..j] when A[i] > A[j], inserting A[j] before A[i]. This preserves left-half order (stability). Time O(n log n); space O(1). Stable like mergesort but without extra buffer; quicksort is typically not stable.
hint: Rotate to bring the next right-half item before a larger left-half item.
mnemonic: ROTATE: Rotate to merge; Order of equals preserved; Time O(n log n); In-place.

front: Earliest Finish Time in a DAG via topological order
back: Process nodes in topological order. Initialize finish[i] = durations[i]. For each edge u->v, update finish[v] = max(finish[v], finish[u] + durations[v]). After processing all nodes, finish[i] is the earliest finish time. Time: O(n + m).
hint: Think of a pipeline: a task can start only after all prerequisites finish.
mnemonic: MAX-PRED + DUR: Finish = max(predecessor finish) + own duration.

front: Circuit-breaker in load balancer with health checks
back: Health checks monitor nodes; if failures exceed a threshold in a rolling window, open the circuit and isolate the node. Recovery uses exponential backoff and gradual health probes. Sliding window or hysteresis prevents flapping between states.
hint: A fuse trips after repeated failures to protect the circuit.
mnemonic: HEAL: Health checks, Exponential backoff, Automatic isolation, Latching circuit

front: KthLargest (min-heap of size k)
back: Keep a min-heap of the current top k values. add(val): if size<k, push val; else if val>min, pop min and push val. Kth largest is heap[0] when size==k, else null. Time per add: O(log k). Duplicates are allowed; only values strictly greater than the min replace it.
hint: The current kth largest equals the heap's minimum after you reach k elements.
mnemonic: K Top, Min on Top

front: DAG project scheduling with unlimited parallelism (topological DP)
back: Use topological order (Kahn's). Initialize earliestStart[i]=0. For u in order: finish[u]=earliestStart[u]+d[u]; for each v in adj[u]: earliestStart[v]=max(earliestStart[v], finish[u]). Project duration = max finish[i]. Time: O(n+m).
hint: Recall that with unlimited parallelism, project duration equals the longest path length in the DAG.
mnemonic: LP = Longest Path; the finish time equals the time of the longest chain of dependent tasks.

front: Two-phase in-place stable sort for composite keys
back: Phase 1: stable-sort by ts in descending order using an in-place stable merge (block-rotation) with O(log n) extra space. Phase 2: stable-sort by key ascending (stable). The second pass preserves the ts-desc order within equal keys, giving key asc and ts desc. Sketch: sortTsDesc(a); sortKeyAsc(a).
hint: First order by ts, then align by key, keeping stability.
mnemonic: TS-Desc then Key-Asc → TS-First, Key-Last (TSK)

front: Word Break Count with Trie and DP
back: Build a trie from wordDict. Use DP/DFS to count segmentations: dp[i] = sum dp[i+L] for any word of length L that matches s[i:i+L]. From i, traverse the trie along s[i:], and stop when no match. Time/space: O(n * maxWordLen). Edge cases: empty s, no matches, very large counts.
hint: At each index, follow dictionary prefixes to accumulate valid continuations.
mnemonic: TRIE-DP: Track Routes In Every edge; DP Counts Prefixes.

front: DAG scheduling with processing and transfer times
back: Cycle-check via DFS/Kahn, then topo order. Earliest finish: f[v] = max_u(f[u] + t[u][v]) + d[v], track argmax for the critical path. End-to-end latency: set w(u,v) = t[u][v] + d[v], run Dijkstra from source (initial cost d[source]).
hint: Topo order first to respect dependencies; latency uses a transformed graph with edge weights t + d.
mnemonic: PDT: Processing Time + Delay/Transfer Time; Topo-First, Dijkstra-Second.

front: Shortest path in DAG via DFS-based topological order
back: Compute a DFS-based topological order, then relax edges (u,v,w) in that order. dist[0]=0, dist[i]=inf; track parent. If dist[target] is inf, return -1 and []. Else rebuild the path by following parents from target to start. Time O(n+m); Space O(n+m). Topo-order relaxations are correct for DAG shorte
hint: Use topological ordering to ensure predecessors are processed before successors.
mnemonic: TOPO: Topological Order Path Optimization

front: Trie with end-node frequency and top-k prefix search
back: Trie with end-node freq. insert(w,d): walk/create; end.freq+=d. search(w): end exists. prefixTop(p,k): DFS from p; top-k by freq in a heap. Time: O(L) insert/search; O(subtree) prefixTop. Space: O(sum lengths). BST/AVL: O(log n) lookup but no fast prefix; Trie memory-heavy; for large vocab, use radi
hint: Prefix path + end-frequency drive top-k choices.
mnemonic: PATH: Prefix traversal, Aggregate top-k, Terminal freq, Heap order

front: Topological DP for makespan in a DAG with parallelism
back: Compute earliest finish times in topological order. For node u, EF[u] = duration[u] + max(EF[p] for p in preds[u]); source nodes have EF = duration[u]. The makespan is max EF[u] over all nodes. This yields the minimum total time with unlimited parallelism.
hint: Process nodes in topological order; a node can start when all predecessors finish.
mnemonic: FAME: Finish = Add duration to Maximum End time of predecessors.

front: Dijkstra with path counting
back: Plan: Dijkstra with path counting. Track dist[v] (shortest distance) and ways[v] (number of shortest paths). Init dist[s]=0, ways[s]=1; others inf/0. For edge u->v with weight w: if dist[u]+w < dist[v], dist[v]=dist[u]+w; ways[v]=ways[u]; else if dist[u]+w == dist[v], ways[v]+=ways[u]. Return (dist[
hint: Hint: on equal distances, accumulate path counts instead of discarding.
mnemonic: DIST-WAYS — Dist shorter (reset counts); If equal distance, add counts.

front: Introsort: in-place 3-way quicksort with rotation-based merge
back: Hybrid in-place sort: 3-way quicksort with median-of-three pivots and a depth limit; switch to rotation-based in-place stable merge for adjacent partitions to preserve ties; use insertion sort for small subarrays (<16). Time: O(n log n) on average; worst-case depth-limited; space O(1).
hint: Depth-limited, in-place partitioning with rotation-based merge.
mnemonic: ROTIS: Rotation merge, Insertion for small blocks, Three-way sort, In-place, Stable.

front: Trie with per-node BST and top-k layer
back: Trie nodes hold a BST (phrase->score) and a top-k layer for ranking. insert/update/delete: O(L log N); queryTopK(prefix,k): O(L + k log k). Memory: Trie total chars plus O(N) for BSTs/top-k. Handles duplicates and ties; prefix ranking beats plain maps; suffix trees have different tradeoffs.
hint: Follow the prefix path to reveal top-ranked phrases.
mnemonic: TPS: Trie Prefix Scores

front: DP-based string segmentation with wildcard matching
back: Let dp(i) be the min cost to cover s[i..n). For each w with i+|w|<=n, cost = number of concrete-letter mismatches between s[i..i+|w|-1] and w; '?' matches any letter with zero cost. Memoize dp and store the chosen w for backtracking. Answer is dp(0) or -1 if INF.
hint: Treat '?' as a zero-cost wildcard and try all dictionary words at each position.
mnemonic: WILD-DP: Wildcards Include Letters, Divide & Plan

front: Load Balancer with Health Checks & Circuit Breaker
back: Use configurable health checks and thresholds; apply circuit breakers to stop traffic to failing servers; add graceful degradation and request queuing as fallbacks to maintain availability during partial outages and prevent cascading failures.
hint: Stop traffic to failing nodes to prevent a wider outage.
mnemonic: HEAL: Health checks, Evict bad nodes, Adaptive degradation, Load queues

front: Three-way partition quicksort (Dutch flag) for duplicates
back: In-place 3-way quicksort: pick a randomized pivot to avoid O(N^2). Partition in one pass into < pivot, == pivot, > pivot; recursively sort only < and > parts. With D distinct keys, depth is O(log D) and time is O(N log D) in expectation; all elements equal end up in the middle bucket with no further
hint: Remember Dutch National Flag with a random pivot to avoid worst-case.
mnemonic: DRIP: Distinct keys, Random pivot, In-place, Partitions

front: Introsort in-place hybrid sort (quicksort + heapsort)
back: An in-place introsort: quicksort with median-of-three and 3-way partitioning; track depth, switch to heapsort when depth > 2*log2(n); use insertion sort for tiny partitions. This guarantees O(n log n) worst-case by avoiding adversarial pivots and fallback to heapsort.
hint: Switch strategies when recursion depth grows, not just for small subarrays.
mnemonic: QHIT: Quicksort, Heapsort, Insertion, Threshold (depth)

front: Dynamic Trie with per-node BSTs for top-k by freq
back: Trie nodes hold a BST of (freq, word) for its subtree; global word->freq map. insert(w,d): update freq; along the path replace (old,w) with (new,w) in each BST (drop if new==0). delete(w)=insert(w,-freq). queryTopK(pref,k): reach pref node and take top k by freq desc, word asc from its BST. Space: O
hint: Prefix query uses frequency ranking along the path.
mnemonic: TRIE-BST: Tally, Rank, Identify, Elevate

front: Trie with per-node AVL for real-time autocomplete
back: Global word->freq map. Each Trie node stores an AVL of (freq, word). insert(w) updates freq along path. delete(w) removes w from all nodes and the map. getSuggestions(pref, limit) returns up to limit items from the prefix node's AVL, by freq desc then lexicographic. Trade-off: fast queries, higher m
hint: Frequency-first ordering at each node speeds prefix retrieval.
mnemonic: FAT: Frequency-At-Node Trie

front: Consistent hashing load balancer (with virtual nodes)
back: Hash ring with virtual nodes and a balanced BST (TreeMap/std::map) maps request hashes to servers. Adds/removes remap only a small portion; lookups are O(log n).
hint: Imagine a circular hash space where requests move to the next node on the ring.
mnemonic: RING: Replicas In Next Gaps

front: Path Trie for editor autocomplete
back: Nodes map char->child; end flag marks a path. insertPath walks chars, creating nodes (O(n)). autocomplete goes to prefix node then DFSs to collect up to maxResults (O(len(prefix) + maxResults)). Treat '/' as a separator to keep matches within a directory. Use per-node map for children. Space: O(tota
hint: Think directories: slash boundaries govern what counts as a match.
mnemonic: PATH — Prefix, Autocomplete, Trie, Hierarchy (slash-boundaries).

front: Self-healing load balancer with health checks
back: Perform periodic health checks to drop unhealthy nodes from the active pool. Track recovery with a circular buffer; on recovery, reintegrate node by gradually ramping traffic, preserving steady request distribution during state changes.
hint: Treat it like a living cluster: drop and slowly reintegrate nodes.
mnemonic: HEAL - Health checks, Eliminate failed, Align traffic gradually, Load balanced

front: EWMA-driven load balancer with dynamic weights
back: Maintain per-server EWMA latency; compute inverse-latency weights and normalize to sum 1; route requests proportionally to weights. Cold-start: give new servers a small initial weight and ramp up over a fixed warm-up period (linear or exponential) to avoid overload.
hint: Smoothing latency guides traffic, not simple round-robin.
mnemonic: RAMP EWMA: Ramp new servers; Adapt with Moving-average-based Proportional weights.

front: Consistent hashing load balancing
back: Consistent hashing maps servers and keys to a ring; on add/remove, only about 1/N of keys remap. Use virtual nodes to improve distribution. Trade-offs: larger rings give better balance but higher memory and slower lookups; smaller rings speed lookups but worsen remapping and skew.
hint: Imagine a hash ring with virtual nodes smoothing churn.
mnemonic: RING — Remap Incrementally, with a Networked Grid (virtual nodes)

front: Hybrid sort: 3-way quicksort + in-place stable merge (64 threshold)
back: Hybrid sort: partitions >64 use 3-way quicksort with median-of-three pivot; <=64 use in-place stable merge of consecutive runs (rotation). Tail recursion recurses on the smaller part, loops on the larger for O(log n) stack; average O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space, stability for small runs.
hint: Small runs merge in place; large runs pivot with median-of-three.
mnemonic: M3P-ROT: Median pivot, 3-way, Rotation merge

front: In-place stable sort (O(1) space, O(n log n))
back: Bottom-up mergesort with in-place merge of adjacent runs by rotating blocks; double run size each pass; stable by preserving order of equal elements during rotations; O(1) extra space, O(n log n) time; cache-friendly.
hint: Use block rotations to merge adjacent runs in place.
mnemonic: BLOOM: Block Rotation In-Place Merges, O(n log n), Cache-friendly

front: Load balancer with health checks and session affinity
back: A load balancer that monitors server health, automatically removes unhealthy nodes, reintegrates them when healthy, preserves session affinity with consistent hashing, and applies circuit breakers for graceful degradation.
hint: Maintain sticky sessions despite servers joining/leaving due to health churn.
mnemonic: HEAL: Health checks, Eject failures, Affinity, Lifecycles reintegrate.

front: Load balancing algorithms: Round-Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash
back: Round-Robin distributes requests evenly. Least Connections routes to the server with the fewest active connections. IP Hash maps a client IP to a single server for sticky sessions. Use Round-Robin for even load, Least Connections for dynamic load, IP Hash for session persistence.
hint: Think of three strategies: even spread, lightest load, or IP-based stickiness.
mnemonic: R-LIP

front: ML-based predictive load balancing
back: ML-based predictive load balancing uses time-series forecasts (e.g., ARIMA or LSTM) on historical request patterns to predict spikes, then scales capacity and adjusts request distribution proactively.
hint: Forecast spikes before they hit to nudge recall of predictive balancing
mnemonic: FORECAST: Forecast spikes, Orchestrate resources, Route traffic, Elastic capacity, Calibrate load, Optimize continuously.

front: Shortest-path DAG (SP-DAG) & DP for counting paths
back: BFS from s marks reachable nodes. Dijkstra from s gives dist[]. Build SP-DAG: edges (u,v) with dist[u]+w(u,v)=dist[v]. Process in increasing dist; ways[s]=1; for each (u,v) in DAG: ways[v]=(ways[v]+ways[u]) mod 1e9+7. dist[] = shortest; counts per node.
hint: Think in distance order and count along the SP-DAG.
mnemonic: SP-DAG DP = Shortest Path DAG, Dist-ordered DP

front: Consistent hashing (load balancer)
back: Consistent hashing maps both servers and requests to a circular hash space. When a server is added or removed, only a small fraction of keys are remapped, reducing disruption and preserving distribution, unlike traditional modulo hashing which remaps most keys.
hint: Imagine a circular ring where each key maps to the nearest server on the ring.
mnemonic: RING: Ring Indexing Keeps Nearest keys mapped to Nearby nodes

front: Consistent hashing load balancer (virtual nodes)
back: Use a hash ring (SortedMap/TreeMap) to place servers by hash. Map each key to the next server clockwise. Add virtual nodes to distribute load evenly. On topology changes, remap only keys to adjacent servers, yielding O(log n) lookups and minimal remapping.
hint: Think circular hash ring where keys map to the next clockwise server.
mnemonic: RING — Remap Incrementally to the Nearest Group.

front: Real-time adaptive load balancing
back: Monitors per-server metrics (CPU, memory, latency, errors) in real time. Normalize scores, compute an adaptive weight from performance trend, and route traffic proportionally to higher-weighted servers.
hint: Think of traffic following a living score that rises for better performers.
mnemonic: RAPID: Real-time metrics, Adaptive weights, Proportional routing, Intelligent distribution, Dynamic updates

front: Maximum overlap of intervals
back: Sweep through all event times: collect starts and ends, sort, then walk the timeline, +1 for a start, -1 for an end, and track the maximum count. Time: O(N log N).
hint: Sweep the timeline to count how many intervals are open at each moment.
mnemonic: S +1 on Start, E -1 on End; remember: S for Start, E for End

front: Round-robin vs Weighted Round-robin load balancing
back: Round-robin distributes requests evenly across servers. Weighted round-robin uses per-server weights to bias traffic toward higher-capacity servers. Use RR when servers are similar; use WRR when capacities differ or you want to bias toward faster/healthier nodes.
hint: Equal distribution vs capacity-based bias.
mnemonic: RR = Even Load; WRR = Weights Determine Route

front: Exponential backoff health checks for load balancers
back: Use health checks with exponential backoff: when a server fails, retry at gradually increasing intervals to avoid thundering herd, reducing cascading failures and allowing time for recovery during partial outages.
hint: Clue: stagger the retries to avoid a mass retry storm.
mnemonic: Mnemonic: BACKOFF = Backoff And Check Keeps Outages From Cascading

front: Trie for autocomplete: insert, search, startsWith (lowercase a-z)
back: Trie node has 26 children and an isWord flag. insert(w) traverses/creates nodes and marks last as isWord. search(w) is true if the path exists and last node isWord. startsWith(p) is true if the path exists. Example: insert 'apple','app','application'; search('app')=true; startsWith('appl')=true.
hint: Clue: a path spells a word; an end flag marks completed words.
mnemonic: EPMW = Every Path Marks Word

front: Binary tree max depth (DFS recursive & BFS iterative)
back: DFS: depth(n) = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)); base 0 for null. BFS: level-order with a queue; depth equals number of levels. Time O(n) for both. Space: DFS O(h) stack; BFS O(width). Edge cases: null -> 0; single node -> 1.
hint: Think in terms of tree height: count the levels.
mnemonic: HEIGHT: Levels Exposed; Depth Tracked.

front: Dynamic Shortest Path with Edge Weight Updates
back: A dynamic shortest-path structure using incremental updates and hierarchical decomposition, based on a dynamic Dijkstra variant. It maintains shortest paths under frequent edge-weight changes with near-O(log n) time per update and per-query.
hint: Combine dynamic updates, hierarchical decomposition, and Dijkstra-style shortest paths.
mnemonic: DIHD: Dynamic, Incremental, Hierarchical Decomposition (Dynamic Dijkstra)

front: Streaming top-K frequency extraction for huge logs (Go)
back: Use a Go streaming approach: read with buffered I/O, count IPs in a map, and maintain a 10-item min-heap of (count, IP). Use sync.Pool for memory reuse and worker pools for parallelism. Python's Counter is easier but not efficient for 50 GB+ files.
hint: Think: keep only the top-10 as you stream.
mnemonic: HEAT: Hash, Extract top-10, Allocate with pools, Time-sliced workers.

front: Sliding-window DP for counting dice-sum ways
back: dp[i] = sum of dp[i-1]..dp[i-6] modulo 1e9+7; use a 6-entry circular buffer; dp[0]=1. Achieves O(n) time and O(1) space; handle i<6 by summing only existing terms.
hint: Use a rolling window of the last six dp values.
mnemonic: Six-Pack DP: remember the last six dp values to compute the next.

front: Staircase DP with 1-3 steps (rolling window)
back: dp0=1, dp1=1, dp2=2; dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]+dp[i-3]. Use mod 1e9+7 for large n. O(n) time, O(1) space by keeping last three values with a rolling window.
hint: The count for i is the sum of the counts for i-1, i-2, i-3.
mnemonic: TRI-bonacci: remember three previous terms (tri = three).

front: Quicksort vs Mergesort (complexity, stability, space)
back: Quicksort partitions around a pivot. Avg: O(n log n); worst: O(n^2) with bad pivots. Mergesort: O(n log n) guaranteed, stable, uses O(n) extra space. Use quicksort for speed in practice; mergesort when stability or worst-case guarantees matter.
hint: Pivot choice drives performance and stability
mnemonic: Q vs M: Quick = speed; Merge = stability and guarantees

front: Min-Heap (array-based)
back: An array-based min-heap: parent of i is floor((i-1)/2); children 2i+1, 2i+2. Insert: append, bubble up O(log n). extractMin: swap root with last, pop, bubble down O(log n). peek: O(1). Space: O(n). Edge cases: empty heap, duplicates, resize. Used in priority queues and Dijkstra.
hint: Remember the index math and bubbling up to restore order.
mnemonic: PUSH-PULL: Push to insert (bubble up); Pull root for extractMin (bubble down).

front: Minimum jumps to reach end in Jump Game (greedy window)
back: Scan once; keep currentEnd (boundary of current jump) and furthest (max index reachable). When i reaches currentEnd, do jumps++, and set currentEnd = furthest. If furthest cannot advance before the last index, return -1. O(n) time, O(1) space.
hint: Track the current jump boundary and the furthest reachable index.
mnemonic: FUR - Furthest, Update boundary, Reach.

front: BFS vs DFS
back: BFS explores level by level with a queue, yielding shortest paths in unweighted graphs (O(V+E) time, O(V) space). DFS traverses depth-first via a stack/recursion, with O(V+E) time and O(h) space. Use BFS for shortest paths; DFS for exhaustive search/backtracking when space is tight.
hint: Recall level-by-level traversal vs depth-first exploration.
mnemonic: Breadth Before Depth

front: Word Break II with DP memoization
back: DP memoization: dp[i] stores all sentences formed from s[i:] using dictionary words. For each i, check every j>i where s[i:j] is in wordDict; for each sentence in dp[j], prepend the current word and join with spaces. Memoize dp[i] to avoid recomputation.
hint: Use index-based memoization and match substrings s[i:j] against the dictionary.
mnemonic: BREAK: Break, Recursively Examine And Keep memoized results

front: Single Unique Element in Array (XOR trick)
back: Sorting an arbitrary array in O(n) with O(1) space is impossible (lower bound for comparison sorts is n log n). If you only need the unique element, use XOR: let u = 0; for (let x of arr) u ^= x; return u; This runs in O(n) time, O(1) space. If sorting is required, best is O(n log n) (merge sort or
hint: Use XOR to cancel paired numbers.
mnemonic: Mnemonic: XOR = 'Cancel pairs, keep lone'.

front: Sort algorithms: QuickSort, MergeSort, TimSort
back: QuickSort: avg O(n log n), worst O(n^2); in-place but unstable. MergeSort: O(n log n) always; O(n) space; stable. TimSort: O(n log n) worst, O(n) best; stable, hybrid of merge+insertion. Use QuickSort for memory/cache, MergeSort for stability, TimSort for real-world data.
hint: Stability, space, and data patterns guide the choice.
mnemonic: QMT -> QuickSort for memory/cache, MergeSort for stability, TimSort for real data.

front: Linked List vs Array trade-offs
back: Linked lists allow O(1) insert/delete at known positions with dynamic memory; arrays offer O(1) random access, better locality, and no extra pointers. Trade-offs: lists require traversal for indexing and have poorer locality; arrays are fixed-size but compact. Uses: LRU, undo/redo, dynamic queues.
hint: Think fast edits vs fast lookups.
mnemonic: LAID: Linked lists Allow Insert/Delete; Arrays Deliver fast access

front: Min-cost palindrome transformation (insert 2, delete 1)
back: DP: let dp[i][j] be the min cost to turn s[i..j] into a palindrome. If s[i]==s[j], dp[i][j]=dp[i+1][j-1]; else dp[i][j]=min(dp[i+1][j]+1, dp[i][j-1]+2). Time O(n^2); space O(n^2) (or O(n) with rolling arrays).
hint: When ends differ, choose the cheaper path: delete the left or insert to mirror the right.
mnemonic: LIDR: Left Delete, Right Insert — memorize the two options.

front: Trie for prefix search (autocomplete)
back: Trie supports insert, search, startsWith in O(k) time (k = word length), enabling fast prefix search for autocomplete. It shares prefixes to save space. Hash maps offer near-constant lookups but poor prefix enumeration. Trade-offs: higher memory per node and more complex implementation; sometimes sl
hint: Think of a branching tree where each path spells prefixes, sharing common branches.
mnemonic: Analogy: a family tree where common prefixes share branches

front: Quicksort vs Mergesort
back: Quicksort: average time O(n log n); worst O(n^2). Space O(log n) for recursion (in-place). Mergesort: time O(n log n) consistently; extra space O(n). Quicksort is in-place; mergesort is stable (preserves equal-element order).
hint: In-place partitioning vs stable merging.
mnemonic: Mnemonic: QP = Quick-Partition, MS = Merge-Stable.

front: Thread-safe LRU Cache (HashMap + DLL)
back: LRU cache with O(1) gets/puts: a HashMap for key->node lookups and a DoublyLinkedList to maintain recency. On access, move node to head; on put, update or insert and move to head; if over capacity, evict tail. Use ReentrantReadWriteLock for concurrent reads and exclusive writes.
hint: Head is hot; Tail is evicted when full.
mnemonic: Head-hot, Tail-cold

front: Dynamic Programming
back: Stores subproblem results via memoization or tabulation, avoiding recomputation and trading space for speed. Plain recursion repeats work; DP reuses overlapping subproblems. Use DP for problems with optimal substructure and many overlapping subproblems; use plain recursion for simple, non-overlappin
hint: Cache results to avoid recomputing subproblems
mnemonic: CACHE - Cache And Compute Handy subproblems Efficiently

front: K-th smallest element in a BST
back: Inorder traverse the BST (left, root, right) with a counter. Decrement k on each visited node; when k == 0, return that node's value. Space: O(h) for recursion, Time: O(n).
hint: Inorder traversal of a BST yields sorted values.
mnemonic: KIO — K-th In-Order

front: Dynamic Programming: Minimum Cost Path in a Grid
back: DP with tabulation: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]); track parent for path. Time O(mn), space O(mn); optimize to O(n) with rolling row. Reconstruct from bottom-right. Use 64-bit ints; handle empty grid and single cell.
hint: Fill the grid left-to-right/top-to-bottom using the two prior cells as options.
mnemonic: PATH: Plan, Add costs, Trace path, Hold results

front: Sliding-window top-K frequent errors (hashmap + min-heap)
back: Keep a hashmap of counts in the last N minutes and a size-K min-heap for top K. For each event: increase count, adjust heap. Expire events: decrease count; remove when zero. Heap uses lazy deletion. Exact O(K) space is impossible; consider Space-Saving/Count-Min Sketch. Time: O(log K) per event.
hint: Use a map for counts and a running top-K heap.
mnemonic: WHK — Window, Hash, Keep Top-K

front: Critical Path Length (longest path) in a DAG
back: With unlimited workers and unit task times, total time equals the length of the longest path (the critical path) in the DAG. Compute in topological order: dp[node] = max(dp[parent]) + 1. Answer = max dp. Time O(V+E), Space O(V).
hint: Focus on the longest dependency chain that determines finish time.
mnemonic: Longest Chain Determines Clock (LCDC)

front: Two Sum with a hash map
back: Scan the array once, storing seen values in a hash map value->index. For each element x, if (target - x) exists in the map, you have a pair. Time O(n); space O(n). Beats the brute force O(n^2).
hint: Check the complement in the map as you scan.
mnemonic: MAP: Make A Pair

front: BFS for shortest path in a directed graph
back: Use BFS from A, enqueue neighbors, and track distance per node. When B is dequeued, return its distance. If the queue exhausts without reaching B, return -1. This yields the minimum number of edges in an unweighted directed graph.
hint: Think in waves: expand by distance from the start.
mnemonic: BFS = Breadth First Sweep — level-by-level exploration

front: Kth smallest element in a BST
back: Use in-order traversal to see elements in ascending order. Recursive DFS uses O(h) space; a counter tracks the kth value. Morris traversal achieves O(1) space via threading (tree modified temporarily). Time is O(k) on average, O(n) worst case.
hint: Recall the traversal that outputs nodes in ascending order.
mnemonic: INORDER: Inorder Neatly Organizes Results; Delivers Exact Rankings.

front: Kth smallest in BST with duplicates
back: Approach: use an augmented BST storing subtree sizes to locate the kth smallest in O(h) time. Without augmentation, perform an inorder traversal (O(n) worst case). For duplicates, count occurrences per value or traverse with counts that include all duplicates.
hint: Think in-order traversal with counts, or track subtree sizes.
mnemonic: SIZE: Subtree Size Indicates k; Duplicates Count Along the Way

front: Top-K frequent actions in a time-based sliding window
back: Sliding window approach: keep a deque of (timestamp, action) to evict old events, a hashmap for per-action counts in the window, and a max-heap to extract the top-K by frequency. Update on each query in O(1) time.
hint: Moving window tally that forgets old actions
mnemonic: SWIFT — Sliding window, Frequency map, Index by action, Top-K via heap, Forget old events

front: Quicksort algorithm
back: Quicksort partitions around a pivot and sorts in place with average O(n log n) time and O(1) extra space, often faster due to cache. Worst-case O(n^2) with bad pivots or sorted input. Avoid by randomized pivots or median-of-three.
hint: The pivot decides how evenly the data splits.
mnemonic: Q-PIVOT: Quick, Partition, In-place, Verify, Optimize, Test

front: Word Break II (DP with memoization)
back: DP with memoization for Word Break II: recursively try all prefixes of s, memoizing results for substrings and backtracking to assemble sentences from cached lists. Worst-case time O(n^3), space O(n^2).
hint: Memoize substring results to reuse overlapping work.
mnemonic: MEMO: Divide Prefixes, Memoize, Overlap-free.
